questions.text: What is the correct order of the first four SDLC phases?
questions.explanation: The standard SDLC order is: Scoping/Planning → Analysis → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.

questions.text: A feasibility study assesses which of the following?

options.text: Whether the system has been tested successfully
Whether the system can be built, afforded, is legal, and will be used
Whether the code has any syntax errors
Whether users are satisfied with the final product

questions.explanation: A feasibility study covers technical (can it be built?), economic (can we afford it?), legal (is it lawful?), and operational feasibility (will people use it?).

questions.text: What type of test data should be used to check the edge of an acceptable range?
questions.explanation: Boundary data tests values at the exact limits of acceptable ranges (minimum and maximum). For example, if valid age is 0–120, boundary tests would use 0, 1, 119, and 120.

options.text: Testing done by developers to check all modules work together
Testing done by end users to verify the system meets their real-world needs
Testing for performance under high load

questions.explanation: UAT is performed by the actual end users of the system. It verifies that the system does what they need in their real working environment, before full deployment.

questions.text: Which SDLC model explicitly includes risk analysis at every cycle?
questions.explanation: The spiral model builds risk analysis into every iteration of the cycle. It is best suited to large, complex, high-risk projects.

questions.text: What is the difference between alpha and beta testing?

options.text: Alpha tests individual units; beta tests the complete system
Alpha is done internally; beta is done by a limited group of external users
Alpha is done by users; beta is done by developers
Alpha uses erroneous data; beta uses normal data

questions.explanation: Alpha testing is performed internally (by the development team or within the organisation). Beta testing uses a limited group of real external users before the full public release.

options.text: Adding new features requested by users
Updating the system to work in a changed environment (new OS, new hardware)

questions.explanation: Adaptive maintenance keeps the system working correctly as its environment changes — e.g. updating for a new version of the OS, or changes to external APIs it depends on.

questions.text: What is parallel running as a deployment strategy?

options.text: Running the software on multiple computers at the same time
Gradually rolling out the new system to groups of users
Running the old and new systems simultaneously until confidence is established
Testing multiple versions of the software at once

questions.explanation: Parallel running operates both the old and new systems together. This reduces risk — if the new system fails, users can fall back to the old one. The old system is only decommissioned once confidence is high.

questions.text: Which legal framework protects an organisation's software from being copied without permission?
questions.explanation: Copyright law automatically protects original creative works (including software code) from being copied or redistributed without the creator's permission.

questions.text: An analyst uses interviews and questionnaires during which SDLC phase?
questions.explanation: The Analysis phase involves gathering requirements from stakeholders using techniques such as interviews, questionnaires, observation, and document analysis.

questions.text: Which ethical consideration relates to AI systems making decisions without being able to explain their reasoning?
questions.explanation: Transparency is the concern that 'black box' AI systems make decisions humans cannot fully explain or audit. This is problematic in high-stakes areas like criminal justice or medical diagnosis.
